title: Spawn flow creates warren run row before burrow provisioning, attaches IDs as each call returns
rationale: The §4.3 composition flow has two HTTP boundaries (POST /burrows then POST /burrows/:id/runs). Creating the warren run_xxx id first lets log lines, error messages, and event payloads reference it throughout the flow without a chicken-and-egg between warren and burrow IDs. attachBurrow was widened to accept partial inputs so each ID can land independently; failure path runs finalize(cancelled) + best-effort burrow.destroy().

title: Spawn reads agent definition from agents table cache, does not re-render via cn at spawn time
rationale: Re-rendering on every spawn would shell out to cn, adding latency to a request-driven path. Operators control freshness via POST /agents/refresh. The cached row already represents a recent cn render output that survived schema validation. SPEC §4.3's 'frozen for the lifetime of the run' requirement is satisfied by snapshotting the cache onto runs.rendered_agent_json at spawn.

content: Burrow workspace seeding writes .canopy/agent.json (full AgentDefinition envelope), .mulch/expertise/<domain>.jsonl (one append per expertise_seed line, grouped by domain), and .seeds/workflow.txt (workflow body verbatim). Deliberately a single agent.json rather than per-section files since harness file-layout expectations are not yet locked — the harness reads whichever sections it needs.

title: burrow_config TOML parsing uses in-house line extractor, not smol-toml
rationale: HttpBurrowUpInput only forwards [sandbox].network onto POST /burrows; the rest of an agent's burrow_config (toolchain, allowed_domains) lives in the burrow profile and isn't exposed via the HTTP route. Adding a TOML parser dependency just to read one quoted-string field would be heavy. A line-based state machine with quoted-string + comment handling is sufficient and stays deterministic on the small, hand-authored configs these are. Swap to smol-toml (already in burrow-cli's tree) if/when the surface grows.

name: spawnRun rollback on failure
description: Any failure between POST /burrows and the final attachBurrow runs through a single try/catch that: (1) finalizes the warren run as 'cancelled' (queued → cancelled is allowed by ALLOWED_TRANSITIONS), and (2) best-effort calls burrow.destroy() if the burrow was provisioned. The original error is rethrown — cleanup never masks the caller-facing diagnostic. Both cleanup steps swallow their own errors so a stale db handle or unreachable burrow doesn't trade one error for another.

content: RunsRepo.attachBurrow accepts partial inputs (burrowId-only, burrowRunId-only, or both). The §4.3 flow attaches each ID separately as it returns from burrow. The repo throws ValidationError if neither field is set so accidental no-op calls fail loudly.

title: Resume-seq dedup happens client-side, not via ?since= on /runs/:id/stream
rationale: Burrow's GET /runs/:id/stream does not accept a ?since= query parameter — only the burrow-scoped /burrows/:id/events route does. The seed (warren-26d2) explicitly says 'restart-recovery at MAX(seq)+1', so warren computes maxSeq via EventsRepo.maxSeqForRun(runId) and drops incoming events whose seq <= maxSeq on the consumer side. Cost: a brief replay of already-persisted events on every subscribe; benefit: no schema/wire change needed and the bridge stays a pure courier.

content: bridgeRunStream writes the row to the events table FIRST, then calls broker.publish(runId, row). This ordering matters for tailRunEvents: a route handler that subscribes-then-snapshots can't see an event the bridge persisted but hasn't yet broadcast (it'll be in the snapshot); it can't see one published-but-not-yet-persisted (publish only happens after append). The seam dedup in tailRunEvents relies on this invariant.

name: tailRunEvents replay-then-live with seam dedup
description: The HTTP /runs/:id/events?follow=1 helper subscribes to the broker BEFORE reading history from EventsRepo.listByRun. It then yields history first (tracking lastYielded seq), then yields live events while skipping any whose burrow_event_seq <= lastYielded. Subscribing-after-snapshot naively would drop events committed in the gap; subscribing-before with no dedup would duplicate the seam events. Both ends are required.

title: bridgeRunStream is a pure event courier — no run state mirroring
rationale: Burrow's per-run lifecycle (queued → running → terminal) is observed by Phase 7 (reap). The bridge does not call repos.runs.markRunning or finalize; it only writes events and publishes them. Keeping bridge concerns narrow lets it run idempotently across restarts (no state machine guards to worry about) and keeps the reap orchestration (close seeds, push branch, copy mulch) in one place. The seed warren-26d2 scope is explicit: 'subscribe, write-through, fan out, restart-recovery'.

title: Reap LWW key is record `recorded_at`, not the spec's generic "ts"
rationale: SPEC §11.A says 'last-write-wins by record ts'; the canonical mulch field is recorded_at (set by ml record). String-comparing ISO8601 timestamps gives correct ordering without parsing.

content: Warren-emitted (non-burrow) events use stream='system' and burrow_event_seq starting at MAX(seq for run) + 1, so reap.completed/mulch.record.*/reap_failed events sort after bridge-written events in /runs/:id/events?follow=1.

name: Reap step error isolation
description: Each reap sub-step (workspace_lookup, mulch_merge, seeds_close, branch_push) wraps in try/catch and emits a reap_failed event with the step name; the warren state-transition (queued/running → terminal) runs unconditionally afterward, so a sub-step failure cannot leave the row stuck in running. Per SPEC §11.A: reap errors do not fail the run.

title: Reap bridges queued→running before queued→terminal
rationale: RunsRepo's state machine forbids queued→succeeded/failed directly (queued can only go to running or cancelled). reapRun.transitionToTerminal calls markRunning(now) when current is queued and outcome is succeeded|failed, then finalize. queued→cancelled is direct (legal transition). Idempotent against runs already terminal: short-circuits with alreadyTerminal=true.

title: cancelRun forwards to burrow without transitioning warren state
rationale: Reap (Phase 7) is the only path that finalizes a non-terminal warren run, because the terminal transition is paired with mulch merge, seeds-close mirror, and branch push. If cancelRun transitioned state directly, reap's isTerminal short-circuit would skip those sub-steps and the operator would silently lose the agent's partial work. The cancel pipeline is: warren cancelRun -> burrow cancels -> burrow emits run terminal event -> bridgeRunStream persists -> reap fires with outcome=cancelled.

content: steerRun requires only burrow_id, not burrow_run_id — the burrow inbox is per-burrow (POST /burrows/:id/inbox), so a queued warren run that has been provisioned (burrow_id set) but not yet dispatched (burrow_run_id null) is still steerable. The message is delivered to the next agent turn on that burrow.

cancelRun has a partial-spawn bypass: when state=queued and burrow_run_id is null, warren transitions queued -> cancelled directly without a wire call (there is no remote run to cancel). Other state combinations with burrow_run_id null are rejected as ValidationError because they shouldn't be reachable through the spawn flow.

name: control-plane operations emit a warren-side audit event
description: Control-plane operations on a run (steer, cancel) emit a warren-emitted system event into the run's event log: steer.sent for steerRun, cancel.requested for cancelRun. They use stream='system' and seq=MAX(seq for run) + 1 (matching the existing reap convention), and publish through the optional broker so live tailers see the operator action inline with burrow's own event stream. The event is only written after the wire call succeeds — failures (validation, transport, BurrowError) leave the event log untouched.

content: Warren's canopy agent.name is the burrow runtime id by convention (e.g., 'claude-code' → claude-code runtime). spawn.ts forwards [agent.name] as HttpBurrowUpInput.agents so burrow can mount the runtime's binary into the sandbox even when the project clone has no burrow.toml. If a future agent decouples the canopy name from the runtime id, plumb a separate runtimeId field through registry/schema.ts and switch the forward source.

name: spawnRun forwards agent identity at up-time
description: provisionBurrow passes agents: [agent.name] in HttpBurrowUpInput so burrow's collectToolchainPaths sees an effective [[agents]] row even when the project has no burrow.toml. The seam is HTTP only — burrow's BurrowsClient.up forwards options.agents into runUpCommand, which merges into burrowToml.agents before profile build. Without this forward, claude-code spawns hit 'bwrap: execvp claude: No such file or directory' (warren-8526 / burrow-55e3).

content: warren's spawn flow forwards agent.name (the canopy prompt name) to burrow as the 'agentId' in POST /burrows/:id/runs. burrow looks up agentId in its runtime registry to pick an executor. Built-in runtimes are 'claude-code', 'sapling', 'codex' (see burrow's knownBuiltInIds). A canopy prompt tagged 'agent' that should drive claude-code MUST be named 'claude-code' — naming it 'hello-bot' or anything else makes burrow accept the run but never execute it (no registered runtime), and the warren reap immediately marks it failed even though branchPushed:true and errors:[]. Custom runtimes can be registered via burrow.toml [[agents]] but the default path is name = built-in id.

spawnRun's projectsConfig + projectSpawn are paired opt-in: when both are wired (production via createRunHandler), the project clone is refreshed via refreshProject() BEFORE provisionBurrow so agents see up-to-date code. When either is omitted, refresh is skipped (back-compat for unit tests that pre-stage their own fixtures). An optional body.ref field on POST /runs forwards through to refreshProject as the target branch/tag/SHA. The refresh runs before the warren run row is created so a fetch failure does not strand a queued row.

name: bridge-claims-queued-running
description: bridgeRunStream calls repos.runs.claimById(runId) on the first observed event, atomically transitioning queued→running so HTTP clients polling /runs/:id stop seeing 'queued' while the agent is actively working. The claim is idempotent (returns null if already running/terminal) and runs once per bridge invocation via a local 'claimed' flag. Reap still owns terminal transitions and its existing queued→running shim (transitionToTerminal in reap.ts) becomes a no-op when the bridge already claimed.

title: Bridge mirrors queued→running but reap still owns terminal transitions
rationale: Original posture (mx-5157ab) made bridge a pure event courier so reap was the sole writer to runs.state — but reap only fires on burrow terminal, leaving runs stuck on 'queued' in the UI for the entire active lifetime (warren-865e). Splitting the responsibility is the smallest fix: bridge claims queued→running on first event (cheap, idempotent), reap finalizes terminal. Keeps reap's special-case bridging through queued→running intact for runs that go straight to terminal without ever streaming an event.

name: reap-failure-cause-discriminator
description: When reap finalizes a run with outcome=failed, it records a failure_reason on runs to distinguish 'burrow accepted dispatch but never started' from 'agent ran and crashed'. Inference rule: warren state on entry to reap — queued ⇒ never_started (bridge never claimed because no events ever flowed from burrow), running ⇒ crashed (bridge claimed on a real event). ReapRunInput.failureReason overrides inference; reserved 'timed_out' for a future deadline-based reaper. Persisted on runs.failure_reason (TEXT, nullable, only when state=failed), echoed in the reap.completed event payload, mirrored in ReapRunResult, and shown next to StateBadge on RunDetail.

name: branchPushed-requires-both-reap-and-sandbox-git
description: An agent run produces a remote branch (branchPushed:true) only when TWO independent things both work: (1) the agent can run git commit inside the sandbox — requires the burrow worktree's gitdir reference to be reachable from inside bwrap (warren-67cc), and (2) warren's reap step transitions the run AND runs git push for the burrow branch (warren-a69a). When debugging 'agent ran successfully but no branch on remote', check both: peek the burrow worktree for commits AND check whether reap fired. Either failing alone produces the same observable shape (run completes inside the sandbox, warren UI says 'running' forever, no remote branch). First dogfood (warren-5h ago) had both working; second dogfood (run_rpe5tvs06bpg, 2026-05-09) had both broken — net: 12m48s of real, validated work (417/417 tests, lint+typecheck green) but zero output landed on the remote.

content: runs.projectId is nullable (string | null) on RunRow — null encodes 'project was deleted while this run existed', a state introduced by the ON DELETE SET NULL FK shape (warren-5f19). reap.ts must guard run.projectId === null before calling repos.projects.get(); when null, mulch_merge / seeds_close / branch_push are skipped because the project clone on disk is gone, but the state transition (queued/running → terminal) still runs so warren rows never strand in 'running'. UI surfaces this as '(deleted project)' on Runs list + RunDetail.

name: bridge-detects-runtime-terminal
description: bridgeRunStream inspects each persisted event for a runtime-terminal shape (kind=state_change, stream=system, payload.type=result for claude-code) and breaks the loop with terminalDetected={outcome} set on the result. The registry layer (server/bridges.ts runWithReconnect) reads terminalDetected and calls reapRun inline. Without this, burrow's infinite poll-based run-stream never auto-closes, so warren runs would stay 'running' forever after the agent exited (warren-a69a). Bridge still does NOT transition warren state directly — reap owns that, consistent with mx-fadaa2.

title: cancelRun reaps inline when burrow returns terminal state
rationale: Burrow's cancel response already carries the post-cancel run state. When that state is in {succeeded,failed,cancelled} (the typical case for a graceful cancel), waiting for an external reap scheduler leaves the warren row stuck in 'running' indefinitely (warren-a69a — there is no separate reap scheduler in the HTTP server build, only the CLI command had reap wired). cancelRun now calls reapRun inline using burrowRun.state as the outcome. Reap is best-effort and idempotent, so failures land on the run's event log rather than escaping the cancel response. This matches the bridge-terminal short-circuit: both paths trigger reap synchronously when they observe terminal burrow state, eliminating the dependency on a poll-based reap scheduler for the common cases.

content: Burrow's GET /runs/:id/stream is an infinite poll loop (events table tail with default 200ms poll interval) and does NOT auto-close when the burrow run reaches a terminal state. Warren's bridgeRunStream therefore cannot rely on natural source-end as the terminal signal — without an in-stream terminal detector or external short-circuit, the bridge polls forever and warren's run state never transitions out of 'running'.

name: no-model-response-discriminator
description: Reap distinguishes 'agent never produced model output' from generic crashed by checking the events table for kind in {text,thinking,tool_use} on stream=stdout. burrow's jsonl-claude parser maps every claude-code assistant content block into one of those three kinds, so absence of all three = no assistant turn ran. Used to surface credential/auth failures (warren-5165 root case: claude-code emits init system event, prints 'Not logged in', exits — no assistant turn) without a brittle stderr regex.

name: acceptance-canopy-mutation-via-cn-update
description: Acceptance scenarios verify spawn-time freeze of runs.rendered_agent_json by: (a) POST /runs to capture a baseline run, (b) running 'cn update <name> --section system --body <drift>' in the canopy source repo (ctx.fixtures.canopyRepoPath) followed by 'cn sync' (or git add+commit fallback), (c) POST /agents/refresh to make warren re-clone+re-render, (d) GET /runs/:id to confirm the old run's renderedAgentJson is unchanged while a fresh POST /runs reflects the drift. ScenarioCtx.fixtures.canopyRepoPath was added so scenarios can drive this mutation; the existing canopyRepoUrl alone is insufficient (it points to the fake github.com URL, not the on-disk source).

name: branchPushed-true-without-commit
description: branchPushed: true is silent on whether the agent committed anything. src/runs/reap.ts:257-265 pushes HEAD without checking commitsAhead; runs where the agent never ran git commit produce branch refs at the base SHA and reap reports success. Witnessed live in run_a98cfx1fantf (2026-05-09): burrow/bur_r9mjn6da9xc9 at main's SHA, ahead_by 0. Operator confirmation bias is real — a branch in 'gh' listing reads as success even though gh compare main...<branch> shows total_commits: 0. Distinguishable run shape (real success: run_agpet4ev6e4a, ahead_by 1) is invisible in the UI without explicit commits-ahead surfacing. Tracked as warren-f3bb (P1).

title: warren prepends agent.sections.system to dispatch prompt
rationale:
Burrow's claude-code runtime (burrow/src/runtime/claude-code.ts:171-188) only feeds ctx.prompt and pendingMessages into claude's stdin — it never reads .canopy/agent.json, so the canopy 'system' body was dead text on disk. composeDispatchPrompt(systemBody, userPrompt) joins them as 'system

---

userPrompt' and spawnRun passes the composed body to dispatchRun. runs.prompt (warren-side) keeps the user-typed input verbatim; only the body sent on POST /burrows/:id/runs is composed. Empty/whitespace system falls back to userPrompt unchanged. Closes warren-1a09 fix A loading mechanism: the commit-only contract baked into canopy claude-code v3 now actually reaches the agent.
